data.title: Is Baxter International (BAX) Pricing In Too Much Bad News After Its Sharp Share Price Fall
data.description:
• Many investors are currently asking whether Baxter International's share price reflects its underlying value, as they watch how the stock is trading today.
  • The shares last closed at US$17.69, after returns of a 13.2% decline over 7 days, a 15.9% decline over 30 days, a 9.3% decline year to date, and longer-term falls of 51.0% over 1 year, 50.2% over 3 years, and 74.8% over 5 years.
  • Recent coverage on Baxter has focused on how the business is repositioning itself and responding to past challenges. This has prompted investors to reassess both risk and potential reward. This context is important when you look at a share price that has moved a long way over several time frames and you are asking whether market expectations have shifted too far.
  • Simply Wall St’s valuation model currently gives Baxter a value score of 5/6, based on how many of six checks suggest the stock might be undervalued. Next, we will break down what that means across different valuation methods, then finish by looking at a broader way to think about valuation that goes beyond any single model.

Find out why Baxter International's -51.0% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Baxter International Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A DCF model takes estimates of the cash a company is expected to generate in the future, then discounts those cash flows back to today to arrive at an estimate of what the business might be worth right now.

For Baxter International, the model uses last twelve months free cash flow of about $350.5 million as a starting point. Analyst estimates and Simply Wall St projections suggest free cash flow could reach around $1.33 billion by 2035, with interim projections such as $1.05 billion in 2026 and $927 million in 2028. Simply Wall St uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model, where the first years are guided by analyst inputs and the later years are extrapolated.

When those projected cash flows are discounted back and summed, the DCF model indicates an estimated intrinsic value of roughly $28.61 per share. Compared to the recent share price of $17.69, this implies Baxter International is trading at a discount of about 38.2%.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Baxter International is undervalued by 38.2%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 50 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Baxter International.

Approach 2: Baxter International Price vs Sales

For a company like Baxter International, the P/S ratio is a useful cross check because it compares what investors are paying to the revenue the business is generating, which can be helpful when earnings are not a clean guide.

In general, higher growth expectations and lower perceived risk can support a higher “normal” or “fair” multiple, while slower growth or higher risk usually point to a lower one. That idea also applies when you look at P/S ratios, not just P/E ratios.

Baxter International currently trades on a P/S ratio of 0.81x. This sits below the Medical Equipment industry average P/S of 2.86x and the peer group average of 5.52x, so the stock is pricing in lower expectations than many of its peers.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Baxter International is 1.21x. This is a proprietary estimate of what a reasonable P/S might be, given factors such as the company’s growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and risk characteristics. Because it blends these company specific inputs, it can be more tailored than a simple comparison with broad industry or peer averages.

Comparing the Fair Ratio of 1.21x with the current P/S of 0.81x suggests the shares are trading below that tailored benchmark.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Baxter International Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let us introduce you to Narratives, which are simply your story about Baxter International, written in numbers like your assumed fair value and expectations for future revenue, earnings and margins, then linked to a forecast and a fair value that you can compare with the current share price to help decide whether you see a gap between price and value.

On Simply Wall St’s Community page, Narratives give you an easy way to anchor that story in a live model that updates when new information arrives, such as earnings results, news on Baxter’s Novum IQ pumps, guidance changes or new partnerships. This means your view does not stay frozen while the facts move.

For Baxter International, one investor might pick a Narrative with a fair value near US$15.00 per share that reflects tougher assumptions, while another might choose a Narrative closer to US$43.95 per share that reflects a more optimistic view. By seeing both side by side next to the latest share price you can quickly decide which story feels closer to your own expectations.

For Baxter International, we will make it easy for you by providing previews of two leading Baxter International Narratives:

🐂 Baxter International Bull Case

Assumed long term revenue growth: 1.95% per year

  • They expect portfolio simplification, cost reductions and efforts to remove stranded costs to support a recovery in net margins and earnings, helped by a new CEO focused on execution.
  • Their fair value of about US$21.63 per share ties back to revenue of US$12.1b, earnings of US$913.6m and a future P/E of 21.5x by 2028, discounted at 9.1%.

With Phase 1 construction tracking toward late 2025 completion, investors now have more detail on how the company expects to deploy capital and organize the move from build out to operations.

For you as a shareholder or prospective investor, the updated timelines and spending plans outline what the next few years could involve for TSX:LAC. Phase 1 construction at Thacker Pass remaining on track through 2025, together with planned capital spending of US$1.3b to US$1.6b in 2026, highlights a capital intensive build-out that is likely to influence the company’s funding choices and balance sheet over the next few years. Operational readiness work for 2026 and 2027 indicates that management is already planning for staffing, commissioning and process ramp-up, which is often where execution risk emerges for first-time projects.

For you, a central question is how effectively Lithium Americas can manage this large spending program while also preparing for eventual operations. From here, you might focus on whether Lithium Americas can keep Phase 1 construction at Thacker Pass on its stated timetable and within the US$1.3b to US$1.6b capex range for 2026. Approach 1: Marvell Technology Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today using a required rate of return. It is essentially asking what Marvell Technology’s future cash generation is worth in today’s dollars.

For Marvell, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections. The company’s latest twelve month free cash flow is about $1.33b. Analyst inputs and subsequent extrapolations see projected free cash flow reaching $6.38b by the 2031 financial year, with a path of rising annual figures between 2026 and 2035. Simply Wall St uses analyst forecasts where available, then extends the series beyond those years using its own growth assumptions.

When those future cash flows, including the ten year projections provided, are discounted back, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of around $83.63 per share. Compared with the current share price of about $89.57, the DCF suggests the stock is roughly 7.1% overvalued, which is a relatively small gap.

For a profitable company, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to think about what you are paying for each dollar of earnings. Investors typically expect a higher P/E when they see stronger earnings growth potential or lower perceived risk, and a lower P/E when growth expectations are more modest or risks are higher.

Marvell Technology is trading on a P/E of 29.22x. That sits below the broader Semiconductor industry average P/E of about 39.36x and well below the peer group average of 71.18x. On the surface this makes the shares look cheaper than many names in the same space.

data.title: Cencora Investors Weigh Governance Stability Against Growth And Risk Questions
data.description:
• Cencora (NYSE:COR) held its annual shareholder meeting, confirming all board nominees.
  • Shareholders approved the company's executive compensation plans.
  • A new independent auditor was ratified to oversee Cencora's financial reporting.
  • Management shared updated financial guidance and recent earnings results at the meeting.

Cencora, a major player in pharmaceutical distribution and related services, sits at the core of how medicines move from manufacturers to healthcare providers. For investors, board continuity and executive pay approval can indicate that large shareholders are comfortable with how the company is being run. The appointment of a new audit firm adds another factor to watch on the governance side, particularly in relation to financial reporting quality.

The combination of renewed board backing, new auditor oversight, and updated guidance offers a more complete view of how Cencora is positioning itself. As you follow NYSE:COR, these meeting outcomes can help shape questions about capital allocation, risk controls, and how management plans to respond to potential shifts in healthcare demand and policy.

For Cencora, this shareholder meeting ties leadership, governance, and financial performance together in a way that matters for anyone watching long-term execution. Re-electing the full slate of eleven directors and backing executive pay keeps the existing leadership team and incentives in place just as the company reports adjusted EPS of $4.08 for Q1 2026 and raises its full year guidance. Ratifying Ernst & Young as the new auditor adds a fresh set of eyes on the accounts, which some investors may see as helpful given recent higher operating expenses and an asset impairment in the U.S. consulting business. The combination of guidance upgrades, a $0.60 quarterly dividend, and continued investment in areas like oncology and European distribution suggests the board is comfortable with the current direction, even as analysts voice questions about the OneOncology stake, pricing changes from manufacturers, and leverage. For you, the key question is whether this leadership continuity and oversight framework feels robust enough for the risks that come with acquisitions, international expansion, and tighter scrutiny across pharmaceutical distribution.

Approach 1: AptarGroup Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A discounted cash flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company could be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to a present value.

For AptarGroup, the model uses last twelve months free cash flow of about $260.8 million, then applies a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on analyst forecasts and longer term projections. Analyst estimates feed into cash flow projections through 2028, including $402.2 million in 2026 and $451.9 million in 2027, with $452.3 million in 2028. Beyond that, Simply Wall St extrapolates further free cash flow out to 2035 using modest step ups each year.

When all these projected cash flows are discounted back to today and added together, the DCF model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $172.06 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $132.08, this implies a 23.2% discount, which indicates that AptarGroup may be undervalued on this cash flow based view.

data.title: Heidelberg Materials Shuts Paderborn Plant As Weak Demand Tests Earnings
data.description:
• Heidelberg Materials has decided to close its Paderborn cement plant in Germany.
  • The closure follows weak construction demand that has put pressure on cement volumes in the region.
  • The move reflects a broader effort to adjust the company’s production footprint to current market conditions.

For investors watching XTRA:HEI, the closure comes after a volatile period for the share price, with the stock at €174.1 and showing a 21.5% decline year to date and an 18.5% decline over the past month. Even so, the longer term picture has been very different, with returns of 197.0% over three years and 177.1% over five years, which underlines how cyclical this sector can be.

The Paderborn decision may influence Heidelberg Materials' cost base, capacity utilization, and regional supply options, which are key issues if construction demand remains weak. As new information emerges on how production is reallocated and how the closure affects employees and local markets, investors may want to watch whether management signals any further footprint changes or updates on demand trends in Germany and neighboring countries.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Heidelberg Materials by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Heidelberg Materials.

📰 Beyond the headline: 1 risk and 4 things going right for Heidelberg Materials that every investor should see.

The closure of the Paderborn plant points to Heidelberg Materials continuing to reshape its production network toward higher utilization and lower fixed costs. With full year 2025 sales at €21,498.2m and net income of €1,940.9m, the group is still generating sizeable earnings, so this looks less like a defensive move for survival and more like tightening capacity where demand has weakened. For you as an investor, the key question is whether reduced local supply in west Germany supports pricing discipline or whether competitors such as Holcim, CRH, or Buzzi benefit by stepping into any regional gaps.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Heidelberg Materials Narrative

  • The closure lines up with the company focus on operational efficiency, as consolidating production into more efficient plants can support the margin improvement ambitions mentioned in the narrative.
  • At the same time, it highlights the risk of persistent volume weakness in Europe, which the narrative already flags as a potential drag on long term revenue growth.
  • The specific impact of plant closures on local market share, logistics costs, and the roll out of low carbon products like evoZero is not fully captured in the narrative and may affect how quickly the company can scale its green offering in Germany.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Heidelberg Materials to help decide what it is worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ The plant shutdown underlines exposure to weak construction demand in mature European markets, which could weigh on volumes if conditions stay soft.
  • ⚠️ Concentrating production in fewer sites can increase execution risk if there are disruptions or if competitors choose to compete more aggressively on price.
  • 🎁 Rationalizing capacity may support better plant utilization and help protect profitability if the company successfully shifts volumes to more efficient facilities.
  • 🎁 The move could free up resources for projects in higher growth regions or for low carbon technologies, which aligns with the company focus on sustainable products and efficiency.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it is worth tracking any commentary from management on how Paderborn volumes are redirected, what one off closure costs look like, and whether there are further plant reviews in Germany or neighboring markets. Updates on pricing in regions affected by the closure, together with any shifts in market share versus peers like Holcim and CRH, will help you judge whether this capacity adjustment supports earnings quality or signals deeper demand weakness.
